feedback.text: In my experience, firms do not respect their clients or their specific rules when dealing with them (EULAs etc.). I have often had to experience how accounts are closed with a ‘reason’ which has to be broken down to a timid moist towards a constantly changing ‘rule’, and I have never ever been accused of a specific breach of the rule. There is either no response to the request, or a (presumably automatic) repeating only what has already been said and does not provide any indication as to why such a measure could be justified. In addition, after contacting about such closures, I had to be completely excluded from the system provided for that purpose, without even having access to any prior correspondence relating to them. In the meantime, I also see more frequent clauses in such ‘rules’ which effectively say ‘we reserve us at any time from taking any action, even if they go beyond this set of rules’, and I cannot be seen with the best will to tolerate such something, it offers no added value and ultimately gives the impression that these firms are allowed to exercise absolute freedom of narrism – in my view absolutely unacceptable. This is all the more so when account-related sales contracts are entered into with such firms. Ultimately, it allows those firms to demand money for ‘services’, but to recall them at any time without restriction, control or accountability (unless the individual chooses to use their own time to take legal countermeasures for such ridiculous behaviour) and to withhold the corresponding monetary consideration. This must be avoided in my opinion, and this opportunity seems to me to be appropriate for that purpose. In my experience, the GDPR has been truly successful. If private data appear on the internet and deletion is requested, this is quite arguably ignored when we attach a reference to GDPR and the right to be forgotten at once, as I would like to see for the above-mentioned behaviour. However, I also find that the principle can be extended beyond purely digital situations (and this should also be done). However, if I acquire a device which is advertised for its functions, it can only be retrieved from the manufacturer’s incredibly restricted mobile app, if I would even be able to use it for myself, but it is not possible for me to use it because manufacturers seem to be entitled to slaughter products and evaluate them in such a way that they are absolutely useless and inaccessible if the manufacturer decides not to develop the application accordingly, simply to no longer support or go bankrupt. If I buy it, I think I should be able to do it whatever I want and not be exposed to the future launs and to the future well-being of the manufacturer and its servers, all the more so in the case of physical devices connected digitally.
